From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Climate characterised by long, usually very cold winters, and short, cool summers

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/K%C3%B6ppen_World_Map_Dsc%2C_Dwc%2C_Dfc%2C_Dsd%2C_Dwd_and_Dfd_%28Subarctic%29.svg/500px-K%C3%B6ppen_World_Map_Dsc%2C_Dwc%2C_Dfc%2C_Dsd%2C_Dwd_and_Dfd_%28Subarctic%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_World_Map_Dsc,_Dwc,_Dfc,_Dsd,_Dwd_and_Dfd_(Subarctic).svg)

*Subarctic climate* worldwide

The **subarctic climate** (also called **subpolar climate**, or **boreal climate**) is a [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") with long, cold (often very cold) winters, and short, warm to cool summers. It is found on large landmasses, often away from the moderating effects of an ocean, generally at [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") from 50°N to 70°N, poleward of the [humid continental climates](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"). Like other Class D climates, they are rare in the Southern Hemisphere, only found at some isolated highland elevations. Subarctic or boreal climates are the source regions for the cold air that affects temperate latitudes to the south in winter. These climates represent [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfc*, *Dwc*, *Dsc*, *Dfd*, *Dwd* and *Dsd*.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

This type of climate offers some of the most extreme seasonal temperature variations found on the planet: in winter, temperatures can drop to below −50 °C (−58 °F) and in summer, the temperature may exceed 26 °C (79 °F). However, the summers are short; no more than three months of the year (but at least one month) must have a 24-hour average temperature of at least 10 °C (50 °F) to fall into this category of climate, and the coldest month should average below 0 °C (32 °F) (or −3 °C (27 °F)). Record low temperatures can approach −70 °C (−94 °F).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

With 5–7 consecutive months when the average temperature is below freezing, all moisture in the soil and subsoil freezes solidly to depths of many feet. Summer warmth is insufficient to thaw more than a few surface feet, so [permafrost](/wiki/Permafrost "Permafrost") prevails under most areas not near the southern boundary of this climate zone. Seasonal thaw penetrates from 2 to 14 ft (0.6 to 4.3 m), depending on latitude, aspect, and type of ground.[[2]](#cite_note-usdf-2) Some northern areas with subarctic climates located near oceans (southern [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), northern [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Sakhalin Oblast](/wiki/Sakhalin_Oblast "Sakhalin Oblast") and [Kamchatka Oblast](/wiki/Kamchatka_Oblast "Kamchatka Oblast")), have milder winters and no permafrost, and are more suited for farming unless precipitation is excessive. The frost-free season is very short, varying from about 45 to 100 days at most, and a freeze can occur anytime outside the summer months in many areas.

### Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

The first D indicates continentality, with the coldest month below 0 °C (32 °F) (or −3 °C (27 °F)).

The second letter denotes precipitation patterns:

* *s*: A dry summer—the driest month in the high-sun half of the year (April to September in the Northern Hemisphere, October to March in the Southern Hemisphere) has less than 30 millimetres (1.18 in)/40 millimetres (1.57 in) of rainfall and has exactly or less than 1⁄3 the precipitation of the wettest month in the low-sun half of the year (October to March in the Northern Hemisphere, April to September in the Southern Hemisphere),
* *w*: A dry winter—the driest month in the low-sun half of the year has exactly or less than one‑tenth of the precipitation found in the wettest month in the summer half of the year,
* *f*: No dry season—does not meet either of the alternative specifications above; precipitation and humidity are often high year-round.

The third letter denotes temperature:

* *c*: Regular subarctic, only one–three months above 10 °C (50.0 °F), coldest month between 0 °C (32 °F) (or −3 °C (27 °F)) and −38 °C (−36.4 °F).
* *d*: Severely cold subarctic, only one–three months above 10 °C (50.0 °F), coldest month at or below −38 °C (−36.4 °F).

### Precipitation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Precipitation")]

Most subarctic climates have little precipitation, typically no more than 380 mm (15 in) over an entire year due to the low temperatures and [evapotranspiration](/wiki/Evapotranspiration "Evapotranspiration"). Away from the coasts, precipitation occurs mostly in the summer months, while in coastal areas with subarctic climates the heaviest precipitation is usually during the autumn months when the relative warmth of sea vis-à-vis land is greatest. Low precipitation, by the standards of more temperate regions with longer summers and warmer winters, is typically sufficient in view of the very low [evapotranspiration](/wiki/Evapotranspiration "Evapotranspiration") to allow a water-logged terrain in many areas of subarctic climate and to permit snow cover during winter, which is generally persistent for an extended period.

A notable exception to this pattern is that subarctic climates occurring at high elevations in otherwise temperate regions have extremely high precipitation due to [orographic lift](/wiki/Orographic_lift "Orographic lift"). [Mount Washington](/wiki/Mount_Washington_(New_Hampshire) "Mount Washington (New Hampshire)"), with temperatures typical of a subarctic climate, receives an average rain-equivalent of 101.91 inches (2,588.5 mm) of precipitation per year.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Coastal areas of [Khabarovsk Krai](/wiki/Khabarovsk_Krai "Khabarovsk Krai") also have much higher precipitation in summer due to orographic influences (up to 175 millimetres (6.9 in) in July in some areas), whilst the mountainous [Kamchatka](/wiki/Kamchatka "Kamchatka") peninsula and [Sakhalin](/wiki/Sakhalin "Sakhalin") island are even wetter, since orographic moisture isn't confined to the warmer months and creates large [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") in Kamchatka. [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador"), in eastern Canada, is similarly wet throughout the year due to the semi-permanent [Icelandic Low](/wiki/Icelandic_Low "Icelandic Low") and can receive up to 1,300 millimetres (51 in) of rainfall equivalent per year, creating a snow cover of up to 1.5 metres (59 in) that does not melt until June.

### Vegetation and land use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Vegetation and land use")]

[Vegetation](/wiki/Vegetation "Vegetation") in regions with subarctic climates is generally of low diversity, as only hardy tree species can survive the long winters and make use of the short summers. [Trees](/wiki/Tree "Tree") are mostly limited to [conifers](/wiki/Pinophyta "Pinophyta"), as few broadleaved trees are able to survive the very low temperatures in winter. This type of forest is also known as [taiga](/wiki/Taiga "Taiga"), a term which is sometimes applied to the climate found therein as well. Even though the diversity may be low, the area and numbers are high, and the taiga (boreal) forest is the largest forest biome on the planet, with most of the forests located in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). The process by which plants become acclimated to cold temperatures is called [hardening](/wiki/Hardening_(botany) "Hardening (botany)").

Agricultural potential is generally poor, due to the natural infertility of soils[[4]](#cite_note-4) and the prevalence of swamps and lakes left by departing [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet"), and short growing seasons prohibit all but the hardiest of crops. Despite the short season, the long summer days at such latitudes do permit some agriculture. In some areas, ice has scoured rock surfaces bare, entirely stripping off the overburden. Elsewhere, rock basins have been formed and stream courses dammed, creating countless lakes.[[2]](#cite_note-usdf-2)

### Neighboring regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Neighboring regions")]

Should one go northward or even toward a polar sea, one finds that the warmest month has an average temperature of less than 10 °C (50 °F), and the subarctic climate grades into a [tundra climate](/wiki/Tundra_climate "Tundra climate") not at all suitable for trees. Southward, this climate grades into the [humid continental climates](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") with longer summers (and usually less-severe winters) allowing [broadleaf trees](/wiki/Broad-leaved_tree "Broad-leaved tree"); in a few locations close to a temperate sea (as in [northern Norway](/wiki/North_Norway "North Norway") and [southern Alaska](/wiki/Southcentral_Alaska "Southcentral Alaska")), this climate can grade into a short-summer version of an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate"), the [subpolar oceanic climate](/wiki/Subpolar_oceanic_climate "Subpolar oceanic climate"), as the sea is approached where winter temperatures average near or above freezing despite maintaining the short, cool summers. In China and Mongolia, as one moves southwestwards or towards lower elevations, temperatures increase but precipitation is so low that the subarctic climate grades into a cold [semi-arid climate](/wiki/Semi-arid_climate "Semi-arid climate").

## Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Distribution")]

### *Dfc* and *Dfd* distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Dfc and Dfd distribution")]

The *Dfc* climate, by far the most common subarctic type, is found in the following areas:[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-KGcc2007-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Kuysumy_mountains_and_Torgashinsky_range._View_from_viewing_platform_on_Kashtakovskaya_path_%28Stolby_reserve%2C_Krasnoyarsk_city%29_4Y1A8757_%2828363120875%29.jpg/250px-Kuysumy_mountains_and_Torgashinsky_range._View_from_viewing_platform_on_Kashtakovskaya_path_%28Stolby_reserve%2C_Krasnoyarsk_city%29_4Y1A8757_%2828363120875%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kuysumy_mountains_and_Torgashinsky_range._View_from_viewing_platform_on_Kashtakovskaya_path_(Stolby_reserve,_Krasnoyarsk_city)_4Y1A8757_(28363120875).jpg)

View of pines in the Kuysumy mountains in [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia")

* **Eurasia**
  + The majority of [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") (notable cities: [Yakutsk](/wiki/Yakutsk "Yakutsk"), [Surgut](/wiki/Surgut "Surgut"), [Norilsk](/wiki/Norilsk "Norilsk"), [Magadan](/wiki/Magadan "Magadan"))
  + The [Kamchatka Peninsula](/wiki/Kamchatka_Peninsula "Kamchatka Peninsula") and the northern and central parts of the [Kuril Islands](/wiki/Kuril_Islands "Kuril Islands") and [Sakhalin](/wiki/Sakhalin "Sakhalin") Island (notable cities: [Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky](/wiki/Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky "Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky"))
  + Northern [European Russia](/wiki/European_Russia "European Russia") (notable cities: [Murmansk](/wiki/Murmansk "Murmansk"), [Arkhangelsk](/wiki/Arkhangelsk "Arkhangelsk"), [Vorkuta](/wiki/Vorkuta "Vorkuta"))
  + The northern half of [Fennoscandia](/wiki/Fennoscandia "Fennoscandia") (milder winters in coastal areas) and higher elevations further south (notable cities: [Oulu](/wiki/Oulu "Oulu"), [Umeå](/wiki/Ume%C3%A5 "Umeå"), [Tromsø](/wiki/Troms%C3%B8 "Tromsø"))
  + The Western [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") between 1,600 and 2,100 meters (5,249 and 6,890 ft), and the Eastern Alps between 1,450 and 1,800 meters (4,757 and 5,906 ft)
  + Central [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania")
  + Some parts of central [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland")
  + The [Tatra Mountains](/wiki/Tatra_Mountains "Tatra Mountains") in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), above 800 meters (2,625 ft).
  + The [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees"), between 1,600 and 2,100 meters (5,249 and 6,890 ft)
  + The [Northeastern Anatolia Region](/w/index.php?title=Northeastern_Anatolia_Region&action=edit&redlink=1 "Northeastern Anatolia Region (page does not exist)") and the [Pontic Alps](/wiki/Pontic_Alps "Pontic Alps"), between 1,600 and 2,100 meters (5,249 and 6,890 ft)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
  + Mountain summits in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), most notably in the [Cairngorms](/wiki/Cairngorms_National_Park "Cairngorms National Park") and the [Nevis Range](/wiki/Nevis_Range "Nevis Range")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
  + The far northeast of Turkey

Further north and east in Siberia, continentality increases so much that winters can be exceptionally severe, averaging below −38 °C (−36 °F), even though the hottest month still averages more than 10 °C (50 °F). This creates *Dfd* climates, which are mostly found in the [Sakha Republic](/wiki/Sakha_Republic "Sakha Republic"):

* [Northeast Siberian taiga](/wiki/Northeast_Siberian_taiga "Northeast Siberian taiga")
* [Central Yakutian Lowland](/wiki/Central_Yakutian_Lowland "Central Yakutian Lowland")
* [Oymyakon](/wiki/Oymyakon "Oymyakon")
* [Verkhoyansk](/wiki/Verkhoyansk "Verkhoyansk")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Subarctic_Tundra.JPG/250px-Subarctic_Tundra.JPG)](/wiki/File:Subarctic_Tundra.JPG)

Subarctic climate in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), near [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon")

* **North America**
  + Most of [Interior](/wiki/Alaska_Interior "Alaska Interior"), [Western](/w/index.php?title=Western_Alaska&action=edit&redlink=1 "Western Alaska (page does not exist)") and [Southcentral Alaska](/wiki/Southcentral_Alaska "Southcentral Alaska") (notable cities and towns: [Anchorage](/wiki/Anchorage "Anchorage"), [Wasilla](/wiki/Wasilla "Wasilla"), [Nome](/wiki/Nome,_Alaska "Nome, Alaska"), [Bethel](/wiki/Bethel,_Alaska "Bethel, Alaska"), [Fort Yukon](/wiki/Fort_Yukon "Fort Yukon"))
  + The high [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") in [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") and the [White Mountains](/wiki/White_Mountains_(New_Hampshire) "White Mountains (New Hampshire)") of [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire") (notable locations: [Fraser](/wiki/Fraser,_Colorado "Fraser, Colorado"), [Brian Head](/wiki/Brian_Head,_Utah "Brian Head, Utah"))
  + Much of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") from about [53](/wiki/53rd_parallel_north "53rd parallel north")–[55°N](/wiki/55th_parallel_north "55th parallel north") to the [tree line](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line"), including:
    - Southern [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador") (notable cities: [Labrador City](/wiki/Labrador_City "Labrador City"))
    - Certain areas within [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") interior and along its northern coast
    - [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"): [Jamésie](/wiki/Jam%C3%A9sie "Jamésie"), [Côte-Nord](/wiki/C%C3%B4te-Nord "Côte-Nord") and far southern [Nunavik](/wiki/Nunavik "Nunavik") (Notable locations: [Inukjuak](/wiki/Inukjuak "Inukjuak"), [Kuujjuaq](/wiki/Kuujjuaq "Kuujjuaq"), [Sept-Îles](/wiki/Sept-%C3%8Eles,_Quebec "Sept-Îles, Quebec"))
    - Far northern [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario")
    - The northern [Prairie Provinces](/wiki/Prairie_Provinces "Prairie Provinces") (Notable locations: [Fort McMurray](/wiki/Fort_McMurray "Fort McMurray"), [Churchill](/wiki/Churchill,_Manitoba "Churchill, Manitoba"))
    - The [Rocky Mountain Foothills](/wiki/Rocky_Mountain_Foothills "Rocky Mountain Foothills") in [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia")
    - Most of the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") (Notable cities: [Whitehorse](/wiki/Whitehorse "Whitehorse"), [Dawson City](/wiki/Dawson_City "Dawson City"))
    - Most of the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories") (Notable cities: [Yellowknife](/wiki/Yellowknife "Yellowknife"), [Inuvik](/wiki/Inuvik "Inuvik"))
    - Southwestern [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut") (Notable locations: [Baker Lake](/wiki/Baker_Lake,_Nunavut "Baker Lake, Nunavut"), [Rankin Inlet](/wiki/Rankin_Inlet "Rankin Inlet"))

In the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), the *Dfc* climate is found only in small, isolated pockets in the [Snowy Mountains](/wiki/Snowy_Mountains "Snowy Mountains") of [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), the [Southern Alps](/wiki/Southern_Alps "Southern Alps") of [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), and the [Lesotho Highlands](/wiki/Lesotho_Highlands "Lesotho Highlands"). In [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), this climate occurs on the western slope of the central [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") and [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), where climatic conditions are notably more humid compared to the eastern slope. The presence of the Andes mountain range contributes to a wetter climate on the western slope by capturing moisture from the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), resulting in increased precipitation, especially during the winter months. This climate zone supports the presence of temperate rainforests, mostly on highest areas of the [Valdivian rainforest](/wiki/Valdivian_temperate_forests "Valdivian temperate forests") in Chile and the [subantarctic forest](/wiki/Magellanic_subpolar_forests "Magellanic subpolar forests") in Argentina.

### *Dsc* and *Dsd* distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Dsc and Dsd distribution")]

Climates classified as *Dsc* or *Dsd*, with a dry summer, are rare, occurring in very small areas at high elevation around the [Mediterranean Basin](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin"); [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"); [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan"); [Tajikistan](/wiki/Tajikistan "Tajikistan"); [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") and other parts of the [northwestern United States](/wiki/Northwestern_United_States "Northwestern United States") ([Eastern Washington](/wiki/Eastern_Washington "Eastern Washington"), [Eastern Oregon](/wiki/Eastern_Oregon "Eastern Oregon"), [Southern Idaho](/wiki/Southern_Idaho "Southern Idaho"), California's [Eastern Sierra](/wiki/Eastern_Sierra "Eastern Sierra")); the [Russian Far East](/wiki/Russian_Far_East "Russian Far East"); [Akureyri, Iceland](/wiki/Akureyri,_Iceland "Akureyri, Iceland"); [Seneca, Oregon](/wiki/Seneca,_Oregon "Seneca, Oregon"); and [Atlin, British Columbia](/wiki/Atlin,_British_Columbia "Atlin, British Columbia"). [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") are exceptions; *Dsc* climates are common in [Northeast Anatolia](/wiki/Northeast_Anatolia_Region_(statistical) "Northeast Anatolia Region (statistical)"), in the [Taurus](/wiki/Taurus_Mountains "Taurus Mountains") and [Köroğlu Mountains](/wiki/K%C3%B6ro%C4%9Flu_Mountains "Köroğlu Mountains"), and the [Central Afghan highlands](/wiki/Central_Afghan_highlands "Central Afghan highlands").

In the Southern Hemisphere, the Dsc climate is present in [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") as a subarctic climate influenced by Mediterranean characteristics, often considered a high-altitude variant of the Mediterranean climate. It is located on the eastern slopes of the central Argentine [Andes](/wiki/Andes "Andes") and in some sections on the Chilean side. While there are no major settlements exhibiting this climate, several localities in the vicinity experience it, such as [San Carlos de Bariloche](/wiki/San_Carlos_de_Bariloche "San Carlos de Bariloche"), [Villa La Angostura](/wiki/Villa_La_Angostura "Villa La Angostura"), [San Martín de los Andes](/wiki/San_Mart%C3%ADn_de_los_Andes "San Martín de los Andes"), [Balmaceda](/wiki/Balmaceda,_Chile "Balmaceda, Chile"), [Punta de Vacas](/wiki/Punta_de_Vacas "Punta de Vacas"), and [Termas del Flaco](/w/index.php?title=Termas_del_Flaco&action=edit&redlink=1 "Termas del Flaco (page does not exist)").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### *Dwc* and *Dwd* distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Subarctic_climate&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Dwc and Dwd distribution")]

Climates classified as *Dwc* or *Dwd*, with a dry winter, are found in parts of East Asia, like China, where the [Siberian High](/wiki/Siberian_High "Siberian High") makes the winters colder than places like Scandinavia or Alaska interior but extremely dry (typically with around 5 millimeters (0.20 in) of rainfall equivalent per month), meaning that winter snow cover is very limited. The *Dwc* climate can be found in:

* Much of northern [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia")
* Russia:
  + Most of [Khabarovsk Krai](/wiki/Khabarovsk_Krai "Khabarovsk Krai") except the south
  + Southeastern [Sakha Republic](/wiki/Sakha_Republic "Sakha Republic")
  + Southern [Magadan Oblast](/wiki/Magadan_Oblast "Magadan Oblast")
  + Northern [Amur Oblast](/wiki/Amur_Oblast "Amur Oblast")
  + Northern [Buryatia](/wiki/Republic_of_Buryatia "Republic of Buryatia")
  + [Zabaykalsky Krai](/wiki/Zabaykalsky_Krai "Zabaykalsky Krai")
  + [Irkutsk Oblast](/wiki/Irkutsk_Oblast "Irkutsk Oblast")
* China:
  + [Tahe County](/wiki/Tahe_County "Tahe County") and [Mohe County](/wiki/Mohe_County "Mohe County") in [Heilongjiang](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang")
  + Northern [Hulunbuir](/wiki/Hulunbuir "Hulunbuir") in [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia")
  + [Gannan](/wiki/Gannan_Tibetan_Autonomous_Prefecture "Gannan Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture") in [Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu") (due to extreme elevation)
  + [Huangnan](/wiki/Huangnan_Tibetan_Autonomous_Prefecture "Huangnan Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture"), eastern [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan_Tibetan_Autonomous_Prefecture "Hainan Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture") and eastern [Guoluo](/wiki/Golog_Tibetan_Autonomous_Prefecture "Golog Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture") in [Qinghai](/wiki/Qinghai "Qinghai") (due to extreme elevation)
  + Most of [Garzê](/wiki/Garz%C3%AA_Tibetan_Autonomous_Prefecture "Garzê Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture") and [Ngawa Autonomous Prefectures](/wiki/Ngawa_Tibetan_and_Qiang_Autonomous_Prefecture "Ngawa Tibetan and Qiang Autonomous Prefecture") (due to extreme elevation) in [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan")
  + Most of [Qamdo Prefecture](/wiki/Qamdo_Prefecture "Qamdo Prefecture") (due to extreme elevation) in the [Tibet Autonomous Region](/wiki/Tibet_Autonomous_Region "Tibet Autonomous Region")
* Parts of [Ladakh](/wiki/Ladakh "Ladakh") (including [Siachen Glacier](/wiki/Siachen_Glacier "Siachen Glacier")) and [Spiti](/wiki/Spiti_Valley "Spiti Valley") regions of [India](/wiki/India "India")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Middle reaches of the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") in [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal"), [Bhutan](/wiki/Bhutan "Bhutan"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), and [Northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India").
* Parts of [Kaema Plateau](/wiki/Kaema_Plateau "Kaema Plateau") (including [Mount Baekdu](/wiki/Mount_Baekdu "Mount Baekdu"), [Samjiyon](/wiki/Samjiyon "Samjiyon"), [Musan](/wiki/Musan "Musan")) in [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Southeast Fairbanks Census Area](/wiki/Southeast_Fairbanks_Census_Area "Southeast Fairbanks Census Area") in [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska")

In the Southern Hemisphere, small pockets of the Lesotho Highlands and the [Drakensberg Mountains](/wiki/Drakensberg_Mountains "Drakensberg Mountains") have a *Dwc* classification.
